title: Revenue Leakage in Stripe Subscriptions: A Founder's Guide
meta_description: The hidden ways Stripe subscriptions leak money — failed cards, proration bugs, trial overlaps — and how small SaaS founders actually plug them.
h1: Where Stripe Is Quietly Losing You Money
body_markdown:
A founder I know — bootstrapped B2B tool, around $18k MRR — found $2,340 of missing revenue in a single afternoon last March. He wasn't looking for it. He was trying to reconcile a weird number in his accountant's spreadsheet and ended up going down a rabbit hole in the Stripe dashboard. Expired cards that had silently churned customers three months earlier. A coupon he'd created for a Black Friday test and forgot to delete. One customer on an old $29 plan who should've been grandfathered to $49 two pricing changes ago.

That's Stripe subscription revenue leakage. And if you run a small SaaS on Stripe, you almost certainly have some.

The frustrating part is that Stripe itself is not the problem. Stripe is probably the best billing primitive ever built for developers. But the defaults are neutral — they're not tuned for your business — and the dashboard surfaces what happened, not what *should have* happened. Those two numbers drift apart over time. Sometimes by a lot.

I want to walk through where the leaks actually happen, in the order I've seen them bite founders, with the specific Stripe settings and behaviors that cause them. No theory. Just the stuff I've personally had to fix or watched friends fix.

## Failed payments are the biggest leak, and Smart Retries isn't enough

Start here because this is almost always the largest bucket. Involuntary churn — cards that expire, get declined, or hit fraud flags — is the single biggest source of Stripe subscription revenue leakage for companies under $100k MRR.

ProfitWell (now Paddle) has been publishing this number for years: roughly 20-40% of all SaaS churn is involuntary, meaning the customer didn't actually cancel, their payment just failed. For a $10k MRR business, that's potentially $2k/mo of revenue you could recover with zero new marketing spend.

Stripe has Smart Retries built into the Billing product. It's on by default now (it wasn't always — if your account is from before 2019 you should actually check). Smart Retries uses some ML model to pick good retry times instead of just retrying every 3 days like the old dunning cron job did. It works. It's better than nothing.

But it is not enough.

The thing Stripe won't do for you, out of the box: send good dunning emails. The default emails are fine. Fine is not good. They look like they're from Stripe, not from you, and they say things like "Your payment could not be processed" in a way that makes customers think it's a phishing scam and delete it. I've A/B tested this with two different companies and plain-text emails from a founder@ address recover roughly 2x more cards than the default Stripe template.

A few specific things to check today:

- Is Smart Retries actually on? Settings → Billing → Subscriptions and emails. You'd be surprised.
- Are you using the Stripe-hosted customer portal for card updates, or making customers email you? The portal is free and takes 10 minutes to set up.
- Do you have a webhook listener for `invoice.payment_failed` that pings you in Slack? If not, you're flying blind on your biggest leak.

One more thing on failed payments — card account updater. Stripe has this feature where Visa and Mastercard automatically push new card numbers when a customer's card is reissued. It costs $0.25 per update but it catches a ton of would-be churn silently. Turn it on. It's in the Radar/Billing settings and most founders I've asked had no idea it existed.

## Proration, plan changes, and the coupon graveyard

This is the leak that's hardest to see because it doesn't show up as churn. It shows up as *less revenue than you thought you had*.

When a customer upgrades or downgrades mid-cycle, Stripe prorates by default. That's correct behavior. But the specific way it prorates depends on a `proration_behavior` parameter that has three values — `create_prorations`, `none`, and `always_invoice` — and if you set up your checkout flow three years ago and haven't touched it, you might be using a default that's costing you money on every upgrade.

Specifically: if you're doing upgrades via the API and passing `proration_behavior: 'none'`, customers upgrading from a $29 plan to a $99 plan mid-month don't pay the prorated difference. They just pay $99 on their next billing date. Free upgrade for the rest of the current cycle. I did this for six months on a side project before I noticed. Rough math, that was about $400 in missed proration revenue. Not huge. But it was literally money sitting on the floor.

Then there's the coupon graveyard. Go into your Stripe dashboard right now. Products → Coupons. Sort by created date. I will bet you a coffee there's at least one coupon in there from a launch promo or an influencer deal that is still being applied to customers who signed up in 2022 and are still on it today.

One founder I talked to had a `FRIENDS50` coupon — 50% off forever — that had somehow ended up in a Facebook group and gotten used 34 times. He'd meant it for like 5 friends. It had been running for 14 months. That's the kind of thing you only find if you go looking.

> The dashboard shows you what's happening. It doesn't show you what shouldn't be.

## Trial abuse, grandfathered pricing, and the stuff nobody warns you about

Trials are a weird one. If you offer a 14-day free trial and don't require a card upfront, you're going to get some abuse. Fine, that's a known tradeoff. But the leak I see more often is the *silent* one: trial extensions given over email by the founder ("sure, take another two weeks") that never get logged anywhere, and then the customer converts at a trial length you didn't plan for, sometimes on a plan that no longer exists.

I've seen this one specifically burn three different founders. You launch a new pricing page. Old plans get "archived" in Stripe, which means new signups can't pick them but existing subs keep running. Cool, that's how grandfathering is supposed to work. But then a trialing customer from the old plan converts after the archive date and ends up on… what exactly? Depends on your webhook code. Sometimes the old plan. Sometimes an error. Sometimes the new plan at the old price. Fun bug to debug at 11pm.

And grandfathering itself is a leak if you don't have a policy. I'm not saying raise prices on everyone — that's a separate debate and honestly I come down on the side of grandfathering indefinitely for small customers — but you should at least *know* how much revenue you're leaving on the table. If 40% of your MRR is on plans that don't exist anymore, and those customers would pay 50% more at current prices, that's a number worth being aware of even if you choose not to act on it.

Tax is another quiet one. If you're selling into the EU or UK and haven't turned on Stripe Tax, you're either (a) not collecting VAT and eating it out of your revenue, or (b) collecting it but mis-categorizing it as revenue in your MRR number. I got this wrong for about eight months. When I finally turned on Stripe Tax in late 2022 my "MRR" dropped by 6% overnight because I'd been counting VAT as revenue. That's not a leak exactly — more of a measurement lie I'd been telling myself — but it's in the same neighborhood.

## How to actually find your leaks

Here's the uncomfortable truth. The Stripe dashboard is built for operating the payment system, not for interrogating your own business. The Sigma SQL product is great if you like writing SQL on a Saturday night (some of us do, no judgment) but it's $0.02 per row and gets expensive fast.

What you actually want is a weekly ritual. Not a tool, not a dashboard, a ritual. Something like:

1. Check failed invoices from the last 7 days. How many recovered? How many churned involuntarily?
2. Look at any subscription that changed plans this week. Did the proration happen correctly?
3. Scan active coupons. Any that should be expired?
4. Spot-check three random active subscriptions. Are they on a plan that still exists at the price you expect?

That's 20 minutes a week. It will catch 90% of leaks before they compound. The first time I ran through this list on a live business I found $600/mo of recoverable revenue in about 40 minutes.

If you don't want to do it manually — and honestly, most founders won't, because it's boring and not urgent until it is — this is roughly the problem [Stripe Revenue Tracker](/) was built to surface. It pulls your Stripe data and shows you the gap between the MRR you think you have and the MRR you'd have if these leaks weren't happening. Not a pitch, just: if you're going to ignore this stuff, at least have something watching it for you.

The leaks are almost never dramatic. Nobody's stealing from you. It's just that a billing system with 200 configuration knobs, run by a founder who's also doing support and shipping features and answering sales emails, is going to drift. Entropy is undefeated. The companies that grow past this stage aren't the ones that set up Stripe perfectly on day one — nobody does that. They're the ones who noticed the drift at $20k MRR instead of $200k.

Abstract concept: a stylized pipe or conduit system carrying glowing liquid currency, with small droplets escaping from tiny cracks and joints along its length, falling into darkness below. Geometric, architectural linework reminiscent of isometric infrastructure diagrams. Subtle grid texture in background. Minimal, premium tech magazine aesthetic, in the style of illustrations from The Economist or Stripe Press covers. Concept art, no text, no logos, no people.
